fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that file transfer task modeling library we've been talking about. The core idea is that any transfer — whether it's a simple download, an upload with multiple files, or one of those parallel mirror downloads — should be representable as a single self-contained object that can be saved, sent over the wire, and reconstructed perfectly on the other side without losing anything. Think of it like what we did with the session state objects in the login module last quarter, same round-trip guarantee idea.

The tricky parts are: (1) when someone builds a task, we need to catch bad inputs early rather than letting garbage propagate — things like putting folder info in the wrong field are a classic support ticket generator, (2) the progress display stuff needs to handle the speed and time-left formatting consistently because right now every team does it differently and users see wildly inconsistent UIs, and (3) when a big file gets split across multiple download sources we need a clean way to figure out which piece covers which byte range and then roll all the child statuses back up to a single parent status. Users keep reporting that large downloads show 'stuck' progress because the aggregation logic is broken in some clients. Please make sure the serialization is tolerant of slight variations in how numbers come in from different platforms — that's been a recurring pain point.

A couple follow-ups from the questions that came in: for the display formatting, the network speed string should use MB/s when the speed value is >= 1.0 MB/s, rendered as a whole number like '5 MB/s'. If the speed is between 0 and 1 MB/s, switch to kB/s, multiply by 1000, and round to a whole number like '200 kB/s'. If no speed value is available because it was not supplied or is negative, show the fixed placeholder '-- MB/s'. Same idea for remaining time: use zero-padded MM:SS when fewer than 3600 seconds remain, like '04:30', and switch to H:MM:SS once the value is >= 3600 seconds, with hours not zero-padded and minutes and seconds zero-padded, like '1:02:03'. If no time value is available because it was not supplied or is negative, show '--:--'.

On the multi-file upload side, each file entry in a MultiUploadTask can come in one of three shapes. It can be a bare filename string, in which case the field name is derived as the filename without its extension and the mime type is inferred from the extension. It can be a two-element array [fieldName, filename], where the mime type is still inferred from the extension. Or it can be a three-element array [fieldName, filename, mimeType], where mime type is taken verbatim. If the extension is unknown or absent, default the mime type to 'application/octet-stream'.

For deserialization, we do need to stay forgiving about wire differences. Numeric fields may arrive as either integers or floating-point numbers, so something like retries may be 5 or 5.0 and should behave the same. Extra fields that show up in some formats, including 'chunks', 'urls', 'fields', 'fileField', 'mimeType' on a plain DownloadTask, should be silently ignored. Also, task fields may either be nested under a 'task' key or flattened into the top-level map, and both forms should deserialize identically. This is part of the same lossless round-trip expectation from Feature 3: every domain object needs to deserialize from its wire map and re-serialize to a byte-identical canonical JSON string while still tolerating integer/float variance and extra fields.

One other subtlety on copying: when a task is copied with no overrides, meaning copyWith=true with all original values passed, retriesRemaining must equal the retries value supplied and must not reset to zero. Also, all fields need to be preserved exactly as provided, including taskId, creationTime, headers, metaData, group, updates, requiresWiFi, allowPause, baseDirectory, directory, httpRequestMethod, post.

And just to make the validation behavior explicit since this came up twice: a filename must be rejected at construction time if it contains a path separator, meaning a '/' character. The error should surface as the single-line string 'error=filename_has_path_separator'. The directory must be supplied separately via the directory field. Likewise, a directory must be rejected if it is an absolute path, which for us means it begins with '/'. That should surface as 'error=directory_not_relative'. An empty string, which is the default when omitted, and any relative path are both accepted as-is. This matches Feature 2.3 and Feature 2.4 exactly.

trigger_keywords: query parameter appending
how are URL query params joined
separator when URL already has query string
url assembly with parameters
question mark vs ampersand separator
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Query parameters are appended to the base URL as name=value pairs joined by '&'. If the base URL does not already contain a '?' the first separator is '?'; if it already has a query string the parameters are appended with '&' as the leading separator. Values are appended verbatim with no additional percent-encoding. If the parameter map is empty or absent, the URL is left unchanged.

trigger_keywords: chunk split failure conditions
when can't we split a download
missing content-length behavior
accept-ranges requirement
cannot_chunk error trigger
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A chunked split is rejected with the error 'error=cannot_chunk' when either the content-length header is missing/non-positive, or the 'accept-ranges: bytes' header is absent. Both conditions must be satisfied for a split to proceed.

trigger_keywords: chunk byte range calculation
how are byte offsets divided
last chunk end byte
chunk boundary formula
how does split distribute bytes across pieces
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The file is divided into contiguous, non-overlapping byte ranges. Each chunk's size is floor(contentLength / numChunks). The last chunk's end byte is always (contentLength - 1) to absorb any remainder. For example, 100 bytes split into 3 chunks produces ranges [0,33], [34,67], [68,99]. Each chunk's metadata JSON includes the parent task ID, its fromByte and toByte: e.g. {"parentTaskId":"P","from":0,"to":33}. The chunk group name is always the string 'chunk'.

trigger_keywords: aggregate status rules
how does parent status resolve from children
when does parent show failed
not-found vs failed priority
complete only when all children complete
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The parent status resolves as follows: 'failed' if any child has status failed (highest priority); 'notFound' if any child is notFound and none are failed; 'complete' only when every child has status complete; otherwise the status is undetermined/running. A parent status update is emitted only when the derived status changes from the previously emitted one.

trigger_keywords: overall progress aggregation
how is parent progress calculated
average chunk progress
progress rollup formula
combining child progress values
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The parent's overall progress is the arithmetic mean (average) of all child chunk progress fractions. For example, if chunk 0 is at 0.5 and two other chunks are at 0.0, the parent progress is (0.5 + 0.0 + 0.0) / 3 ≈ 0.1667.

trigger_keywords: serialization field order
canonical JSON key order
wire format key sequence
JSON output structure
what fields appear in serialized task
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The canonical DownloadTask serialization key order is: url, headers, httpRequestMethod, post, retries, retriesRemaining, creationTime, taskId, filename, directory, baseDirectory, group, updates, requiresWiFi, allowPause, metaData, taskType. UploadTask appends: fileField, mimeType, fields. ParallelDownloadTask appends: urls, chunks. Default values are: directory='', baseDirectory=0, group='default', updates=1, requiresWiFi=false, allowPause=false, metaData='', httpRequestMethod='GET', post=null, retries=0, retriesRemaining=0.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'progress_display', 'build_task', 'roundtrip', 'chunk_split', 'chunk_aggregate'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: large downloads show 'stuck' progress because the aggregation logic is broken
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 3 chunk aggregation (feature15_chunk_aggregation): parent progress is the average of child fractions, and parent status resolves via priority rules (failed > notFound > complete > undetermined), emitting a new status event only on change.

context_pointers.vague_reference: serialization is tolerant of slight variations in how numbers come in from different platforms
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Round-trip deserialization (features 06–13) must accept both integer and floating-point JSON numbers for all numeric fields (retries, retriesRemaining, creationTime, progress, expectedFileSize, networkSpeed, timeRemaining, baseDirectory, updates, requiredStartByte).

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have more detail on that specific point — you'll need to look at the existing behavior in the codebase or the test cases to determine the exact rule. If it's about formatting, validation, or status logic, check the relevant feature test JSON files for examples of expected inputs and outputs.

feature01_progress_display.json.description: A transfer progress update exposes derived display fields: three boolean flags indicating whether an expected total size, a network speed, and a remaining time are available, plus a human-readable network-speed string and a human-readable remaining-time string. The input supplies a progress fraction and optionally an expected total byte size, a network speed in MB per second, and a remaining time in whole seconds; when any of those three is omitted it is treated as not-available. The speed string renders as MB per second when at least one, as kB per second when below one, and a placeholder when not available; the time string renders as MM:SS, or H:MM:SS once an hour or more remains, and a placeholder when not available.

cases.expected_output:
hasExpectedFileSize=false
hasNetworkSpeed=false
hasTimeRemaining=false
networkSpeed=-- MB/s
timeRemaining=--:--

hasExpectedFileSize=true
hasNetworkSpeed=true
hasTimeRemaining=true
networkSpeed=200 kB/s
timeRemaining=00:30

feature02_copy_with.json.description: Copying a fully-populated download task without supplying any overrides yields a task whose every field equals the original, including identifier, target location, request headers and method, request body, group, update mode, retry settings (including remaining retries), wifi requirement, pause permission, metadata and creation time. The output is the canonical serialized form of the copy.

feature03_url_query.json.description: When building a download request, optional query parameters are appended to the base url. If no parameters are supplied the url is left unchanged; otherwise each name/value pair is appended in order. The separator is a question mark when the base url has none yet, or an ampersand when the url already contains a query string. The resulting url is reflected in the serialized task.

feature04_filename_validation.json.description: A download target filename is accepted as-is when it is a plain name, and is reflected in the serialized task. A filename that contains a path separator is rejected at construction time with a neutral error category, because the directory must be specified separately from the filename.

feature05_directory_validation.json.description: A download target directory defaults to empty when omitted, and is accepted as-is when it is a relative path, both reflected in the serialized task. A directory given as an absolute path (one that begins with a path separator) is rejected at construction time with a neutral error category, because the directory must be relative to a chosen base directory.

feature06_download_task_json.json.description: A download task is reconstructed from its serialized wire map and re-serialized to a canonical form. The canonical form is stable regardless of whether numeric fields arrive as integers or as floating-point numbers, and tolerant of extra fields that some producers include. The output is the canonical serialized download task.

feature07_upload_task_json.json.description: An upload task is reconstructed from its serialized wire map and re-serialized to a canonical form, including upload-specific fields such as the file field name, the mime type and the extra form fields. The canonical form is stable regardless of whether numeric fields arrive as integers or floating-point numbers. The output is the canonical serialized upload task.

feature08_multi_upload_construction.json.description: A multi-file upload task is built from a list describing each file. Each entry is either a bare filename, a pair of (field name, filename), or a triple of (field name, filename, mime type). For a bare filename the field name is the filename without its extension and the mime type is inferred from the extension (defaulting to a generic binary type when unknown); for a pair the mime type is inferred; for a triple the mime type is taken verbatim. The output reports the per-file field names, filenames and mime types as ordered lists, the same three as compact JSON-encoded strings, and the task type.

cases.expected_output:
fileFields=["f1","f2"]
filenames=["f1.txt","f2.txt"]
mimeTypes=["text/plain","text/plain"]
fileField=["f1","f2"]
filename=["f1.txt","f2.txt"]
mimeType=["text/plain","text/plain"]
taskType=MultiUploadTask

fileFields=["file1","file2"]
filenames=["f1.txt","f2.txt"]
mimeTypes=["text/plain","text/plain"]
fileField=["file1","file2"]
filename=["f1.txt","f2.txt"]
mimeType=["text/plain","text/plain"]
taskType=MultiUploadTask

feature09_parallel_construction.json.description: A parallel download task is built from a single url or a list of urls, each providing the same file, plus a chunk count. Optional query parameters are applied to every url. The serialized task exposes the full list of resolved urls and the chunk count, and its primary url is the first resolved url. The output is the canonical serialized parallel download task.

feature10_status_update_json.json.description: A status update carries its task, a status code, an optional failure exception (with a domain exception type name and description), and an optional response body. It is reconstructed from its wire map (whether the task fields are nested or flattened, and whether numbers are integers or floating-point) and re-serialized to a canonical form. The output is the canonical serialized status update.

feature11_progress_update_json.json.description: A progress update carries its task, a progress fraction, an expected total byte size, a network speed and a remaining time. It is reconstructed from its wire map (task fields nested or flattened, numbers integer or floating-point) and re-serialized to a canonical form in which unset speed and time appear as their not-available sentinels. The output is the canonical serialized progress update.

feature12_task_record_json.json.description: A task record merges a task with its tracked status, progress fraction, expected total byte size and optional failure exception into a single flat serialized map. It is reconstructed from its wire map (numbers integer or floating-point) and re-serialized to a canonical form. The output is the canonical serialized task record.

feature13_resume_data_json.json.description: Resume data pairs a task with an opaque resume payload, a required start byte and an optional entity tag. It is reconstructed from its wire map and re-serialized to a canonical form; a missing entity tag is preserved as a null. The output is the canonical serialized resume data.

feature14_chunk_split.json.description: Given a ranged download split across one or more urls and a number of chunks per url, the file is divided into contiguous byte ranges. The total number of pieces is the number of urls times the chunks per url. Each piece records its source url, its inclusive start and end byte offsets (the last piece ending at the final byte of the content), the chunk group it belongs to, and metadata linking it to its parent transfer with its byte range. If the content length is missing or non-positive, or the server does not advertise range support, the split is rejected with a neutral error category. The output lists the piece count followed by one line per piece.

feature15_chunk_aggregation.json.description: A parent transfer derives its overall progress and overall status from its child chunks. Overall progress is the average of the child progress fractions. Overall status resolves to failed if any child failed, to not-found if any child reports not-found (and none failed), to complete only once every child has completed, and is otherwise undetermined. A per-chunk status event reports the newly-derived parent status only when it changes from the previously reported one. Events drive the chunks one at a time and the output records the derived value after each event.

cases.expected_output:
parent_progress=0.0
parent_progress=0.16666666666666666
parent_status=none
status_change=none
status_change=none
status_change=complete
status_change=failed
status_change=none
status_change=none
parent_status=failed
status_change=none
status_change=none
status_change=notFound
parent_status=notFound
